perturbation.original_text: A Party defaulting under this Agreement will reimburse the other Party for any loss resulting from disclosure or unlawful use of Confidential Information. Losses will be reimbursed according to the law of Singapore .
perturbation.changed_text: A Party defaulting under this Agreement may, at the Discloser's discretion, reimburse the other Party for direct losses resulting from intentional disclosure or unlawful use of Confidential Information. Reimbursement amounts will not exceed SGD 1000.
perturbation.explanation: Limits reimbursement for breach of confidentiality to 'direct losses' caused by 'intentional' disclosure and caps the amount. This effectively eliminates liability for indirect, consequential, or unintentional disclosures, and sets a maximum amount, contradicting Singapore law which allows for a broader range of damages.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Singapore Contract Law - Damages for Breach of Contract
perturbation.law_citation: Section 74 of the Singapore Contract Law
perturbation.law_explanation: Singapore contract law allows for the recovery of damages beyond just 'direct losses' and does not inherently limit the amount recoverable to a fixed value like SGD 1000. By restricting the recoverable losses, the modified clause contradicts Singapore law.

Previous Next Ch. 08 The Law of Contract SECTION 1 GENERAL APPLICATION A. Singapore contract law largely based on English contract law 8.1.1 Contract law in Singapore is largely based on the common law of contract in England. Hence, the rules developed in the Singapore courts do bear a very close resemblance to those developed under English common law. Indeed, where there is no Singapore authority specifically on point, it will usually be assumed that the position will, in the first instance, be no different from that in England. Unlike its neighbours Malaysia and Brunei, following Independence in 1965, Singapore’s Parliament made no attempt to codify Singapore’s law of contract. Accordingly, much of the law of contract in Singapore remains in the form of judge-made rules. B. Certain aspects of Singapore contract law based on specific statutes 8.1.2 In some circumstances, these judge-made rules have been modified by specific statutes. Many of these statutes are English in origin. To begin with, 13 English commercial statutes have been incorporated as part of the Statutes of the Republic of Singapore by virtue of s 4 of the Application of English Law Act (Cap 7A, 1993 Rev Ed). These are listed in Part II of the First Schedule of this Act. Other statutes, eg the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act (Cap 53B, 2002 Rev Ed), are modelled upon English statutes. There are also other areas where statutory development based on non-English models has taken place, eg the Consumer Protection (Fair Trading) Act (Cap 52A, 2004 Rev Ed) (which was largely drawn from fair trading legislation enacted in Alberta and Sasketchewan). SECTION 2 OFFER AND ACCEPTANCE A. Contract is essentially agreement between two or more parties 8.2.1 A contract is essentially an agreement between two or more parties, the terms of which affect their respective rights and obligations which are enforceable at law. Whether the parties have reached agreement, or a meeting of the minds, is objectively ascertained from the facts. The concepts of offer and acceptance provide in many, albeit not all, cases the starting point for analysing whether agreement has been reached. B. Offer 1. Definition of offer 8.2.2 An offer is a promise, or other expression of willingness, by the ‘offeror’ to be bound on certain specified terms upon the unqualified acceptance of these terms by the person to whom the offer is made (the ‘offeree’). Provided the other formation elements (ie consideration and intention to create legal relations) are present, the acceptance of an offer results in a valid contract. 2. Offer must be made with objectively ascertained intention to be bound 8.2.3 Whether any particular statement amounts to an offer depends on the intention with which it is made. An offer must be made with the intention to be bound. On the other hand, if a person is merely soliciting offers or requesting for information, without any intention to be bound, at best, he or she would be making an invitation to treat. Under the objective test, a person may be said to have made an offer if his or her statement (or conduct) induces a reas
perturbation.justification: The modified text limits reimbursement for breach of confidentiality to 'direct losses' resulting from 'intentional' disclosure, and caps the amount at SGD 1000. This contradicts Singapore law because, as the Law Explanation states, Singapore contract law allows for broader recovery of damages and does not inherently limit the amount recoverable. Scraped Law Snippet 2 mentions that Singapore contract law is largely based on English common law and that much of the law of contract in Singapore remains in the form of judge-made rules, implying that damages are not strictly limited as the modified text suggests. The original text allows for reimbursement for 'any loss,' while the modified text narrows it significantly.

perturbation.original_text: All disputes arising out of or in connection with this Agreement shall be finally settled under the Rules of Arbitration of the International Chamber of Commerce by 3 (three) arbitrators appointed in accordance with the said Rules. The seat, or legal pla ce, of arbitration shall be Singapore , Singapore .
perturbation.changed_text: All disputes arising out of or in connection with this Agreement shall be settled through mediation, as determined suitable by the Discloser. The location of mediation will be at the Discloser's discretion.
perturbation.explanation: Changes the dispute resolution mechanism from binding arbitration under ICC rules to discretionary mediation at the Discloser's chosen location. This removes the Recipient's right to independent arbitration, potentially contradicting Singapore's International Arbitration Act which upholds the enforcement of arbitration agreements.
perturbation.law_citation: Section 3 of the International Arbitration Act (IAA)
perturbation.law_explanation: The Singapore International Arbitration Act (IAA) supports and enforces agreements to arbitrate. By replacing arbitration with discretionary mediation, the modified clause undermines the Recipient's right to binding arbitration, potentially rendering the dispute resolution mechanism unenforceable under Singapore law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 9 U.S. Code § 3 - Stay of proceedings where issue therein referable to arbitration | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 9 U.S. Code § 3 - Stay of proceedings where issue therein referable to arbitration U.S. Code Notes prev | next If any suit or proceeding be brought in any of the courts of the United States upon any issue referable to arbitration under an agreement in writing for such arbitration, the court in which such suit is pending, upon being satisfied that the issue involved in such suit or proceeding is referable to arbitration under such an agreement, shall on application of one of the parties stay the trial of the action until such arbitration has been had in accordance with the terms of the agreement, providing the applicant for the stay is not in default in proceeding with such arbitration. (July 30, 1947, ch. 392, 61 Stat. 670 .) Derivation Act Feb. 12, 1925, ch. 213, § 3, 43 Stat. 883 . U.S. Code Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities How current is this?
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandated arbitration under ICC rules, but the changed version replaces this with discretionary mediation. The Law Explanation states this undermines the Recipient's right to binding arbitration and potentially renders the dispute resolution mechanism unenforceable. The Perturbation Explanation clarifies that the recipient's right to independent arbitration has been removed and may contradict Singapore's International Arbitration Act. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is from U.S. Code, it references the stay of proceedings 'where issue therein referable to arbitration under an agreement in writing for such arbitration'. Although the US code is not directly applicable, the perturbed contract actively moves away from a clearly defined arbitration agreement, further suggesting a contradiction, because the new mediation is at the Discloser's discretion and not under a mutually agreed-upon process. The absence of a second, useful snippet does not invalidate this since the Law and Perturbation explanations strongly indicate an enforceable arbitration agreement is being undermined.

perturbation.original_text: In the event of a breach or threatened breach, Discloser has the right to seek injunctive relief (in addition to any and all other remedies available at law or equity) without the need to post a bond or other security or demonstrate the confidential nature of its Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event of a material breach, the Discloser may seek injunctive relief; however, the Discloser must demonstrate irreparable harm and post a bond of SGD 50,000.
perturbation.explanation: Adds conditions to the Discloser's right to seek injunctive relief, requiring demonstration of 'irreparable harm' and posting a bond. This makes obtaining injunctive relief more difficult, potentially contradicting principles of equity in Singapore law which may grant injunctions more readily to protect confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Singapore Law on Injunctions for Breach of Confidence
perturbation.law_citation: General Principles of Equity Law in Singapore
perturbation.law_explanation: While Singapore law allows for bonds in injunctions, the original clause explicitly waives it. By mandating a bond and requiring a higher burden of proof ('irreparable harm'), the changed text imposes additional hurdles, making it harder for the Discloser to obtain an injunction compared to what Singapore law may otherwise permit to protect confidential information.

perturbation.justification: The original text allows the Discloser to seek injunctive relief without posting a bond or demonstrating the confidential nature of its information. The changed text adds conditions, requiring a demonstration of 'irreparable harm' and posting a bond. This contradicts the original agreement by imposing additional hurdles for the Discloser to obtain an injunction, making it more difficult. The Law Explanation also states that the original clause explicitly waives the bond, and the changed text mandates it along with a higher burden of proof.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient’s obligations under this Agreement end five (5) years after the date of first disclosure hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient’s obligations under this Agreement end one (1) year after the date of first disclosure hereunder, and only for information explicitly marked as confidential.
perturbation.explanation: Reduces the duration of confidentiality obligations from five years to one, and limits it to information explicitly marked as confidential. This significantly weakens the protection of confidential information, potentially contradicting what is considered a reasonable duration and level of protection under Singapore law, particularly considering the nature of the confidential information involved (implementation of a service for receiving compensation for delayed and canceled flights).
perturbation.contradicted_law: Singapore Law on Breach of Confidence (Reasonable Expectations)
perturbation.law_citation: Duty of Confidence in Singapore Law
perturbation.law_explanation: Singapore law considers the 'reasonable expectations' of the parties when determining a breach of confidence. Shortening the duration to one year and requiring explicit marking may be deemed unreasonable given the nature of the information and the potential for long-term competitive disadvantage, thereby undermining the Discloser's legitimate expectations of confidentiality under Singapore law.

perturbation.justification: The original contract text stipulates a five-year confidentiality obligation, while the changed text reduces this to one year and adds a requirement for explicit marking. The Law Explanation argues this change may be deemed unreasonable, undermining the discloser's legitimate expectations of confidentiality under Singapore law given the nature of the information involved. The Perturbation Explanation notes this weakens the protection of confidential information. The change directly reduces the duration and scope of confidentiality, creating a legal contradiction because it arguably undermines the 'reasonable expectations' of confidentiality under Singapore law. The provided snippets do not directly address confidential information, but the Law Explanation's reasonable expectation argument aligns with the overall legal principles of contractual fairness and good faith.
